notes.anchor: had seized Ariminum, Pisaurum, Ancona, and Arretium
notes.note: The first towns Caesar occupied after crossing the Rubicon in January 49 BC, as his columns spread down the Adriatic coast and into Etruria; their rapid fall is what panicked the Pompeians into abandoning Rome.

notes.anchor: for these have drawn them by lot
notes.note: Domitius and Considius Nonianus had been allotted the two Gallic provinces as their commands; Caesar's terms proposed simply letting them take up the posts.
The trinundinum, the legally required notice period of three successive nundinae (market-days, held every eighth day) during which a candidate had to be personally present in Rome before an election.

notes.anchor: both of which he holds as bitterly hostile (except for the Transpadanes)
notes.note: Cicero claims the Gallic provinces resented Caesar's rule, save the Transpadanes -- the inhabitants north of the Po, to whom Caesar had championed the grant of full Roman citizenship and who were therefore loyal to him.

notes.anchor: six legions and great auxiliary forces, under Afranius and Petreius
notes.note: The Pompeian army in Spain, commanded by Caesar's enemies; Caesar would defeat it at Ilerda later in 49 BC.

notes.anchor: T. Labienus, was unwilling to be a partner in his crime
notes.note: Titus Labienus, Caesar's most senior and trusted legate throughout the Gallic War, defected to Pompey at the outbreak of the civil war; Cicero treats it as a major blow to Caesar's cause.

notes.anchor: in charge of the coastline beginning from Formiae
notes.note: Cicero was assigned the supervision of the Campanian coast as his civil-war command; he deliberately kept it small so as to preserve credibility as an advocate of peace.

notes.anchor: our Dolabella is on Caesar's side
notes.note: Publius Cornelius Dolabella, Cicero's son-in-law (married to Tullia), had joined Caesar -- an awkwardness Cicero registers ruefully even as he sides with Pompey.

notes.anchor: The fourth day before the Kalends of February, Capua
notes.note: 29 January 49 BC, written at Capua. Roman dates count inclusively back from the Kalends (1st) of the following month.
